question.prompt: What, especially, caused Snow to present his argument about “the two cultures” in the first place, according to the text?

options.text: He was concerned about the constant misunderstanding between them.
He viewed the schism between them as relevant in an urgent political context.
He saw little role for science in bridging the gap between rich and poor.
He had a low opinion of the intellectual quality of some humanistic research.

question.context:
The Two Cultures

Few literary phrases have had such an enduring afterlife as “the two cultures”, coined by C. P. Snow to describe what he saw as a dangerous schism between scientists and literary intellectuals, i.e. between the sciences and the humanities. Yet nowadays few people actually seem to have read Snow’s book bearing that title. Why bother when its main point seems so obvious? It was in 1959 that Snow, an English physicist, delivered a lecture at Cambridge called “The Two Cultures and the Scientific Revolution,” later published in book form. Snow’s famous lament was that “the intellectual life of the whole of Western society is increasingly being split into two polar groups,” consisting of scientists, on the one hand, and literary scholars, on the other. Snow largely blamed literary types for this “gulf of mutual incomprehension.” These intellectuals, Snow asserted, were shamefully unembarrassed about not grasping, say, the second law of thermodynamics – even though asking if someone knows it, he writes, “is about the scientific equivalent of: Have you read a work of Shakespeare’s?” Since then, “the two cultures” has almost become a bumper-sticker phrase. There is nothing wrong with referring to Snow’s idea, of course. His view that education should not be too specialized remains broadly persuasive. But it is misleading to imagine Snow as the eagle-eyed anthropologist of a fractured intelligentsia, rather than an evangelist of our technological future. The deeper point of The Two Cultures is not that we have two cultures. It is that science, above all, will keep us prosperous and secure. Snow’s expression of this optimism is dated, yet his thoughts about progress are more relevant today than his cultural typologies. After all, Snow’s descriptions of the two cultures are not exactly subtle. Scientists, he asserts, have “the future in their bones,” while “the traditional culture responds by wishing the future did not exist.” Scientists, he adds, are morally “the soundest group of intellectuals,” while literary ethics are more suspect. Literary culture has “temporary periods” of moral failure, he argues, quoting a scientist friend who mentions the fascist proclivities of Ezra Pound, William Butler Yeats and Wyndham Lewis. Snow’s essay provoked a roaring, ad hominem response from the Cambridge literary critic F. R. Lewis

and a more measured one from Lionel Trilling. Snow’s cultural tribalism, Trilling argued, impaired the “possibility of rational discourse.” So why did Snow think the supposed gulf between the two cultures was such a problem? Because, in his view, it leads many capable minds to ignore science as a vocation, which prevents us from solving the world’s “main issue,” the wealth gap caused by industrialization, which threatens global stability. This brings The Two Cultures to its ultimate concern, which has less to do with intellectual life than with geopolitics. If the democracies don’t modernize undeveloped countries, Snow argues, “the Communist countries will,” leaving the “West an enclave in a different world.” Only by erasing the gap between the two cultures can we ensure wealth and self-government, he writes. Some of this sounds familiar; for decades we have regarded science as crucial to global competitiveness. But in other ways The Two Cultures remains irretrievably a cold war document. Yet Snow’s book actually embodies one of the deepest tensions in our ideas about progress. Snow wants to believe the sheer force of science cannot be restrained, that it will change the world – for the better – without a guiding hand. The Industrial Revolution, he writes, occurred “without anyone,” including intellectuals, “noticing what was happening.” But at the same time he argues that 20th-century progress was being stymied by the indifference of poets and novelists. So which is it? Is science an irrepressible agent of change, or does it need top-down direction? This question is the aspect of Snow’s book that speaks most directly to us today. Your answer probably determines how widely and deeply you think we need to spread scientific knowledge. Do we need to produce more scientists and engineers to fight climate change? Do we need broader public understanding of the issue to support governmental action? Or do we need something else? The separateness of Snow’s two cultures is a very slippery thing. For all the book’s continuing interest, we should spend less time merely citing The Two Cultures, and more time genuinely reconsidering it.

Peter Dizikes, The New York Times Book Review

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to take 'gulf of mutual incomprehension' at face value as the main concern.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage explicitly says Snow's 'ultimate concern' is geopolitical — the gulf is the proximate concern, not the deepest.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'wealth gap caused by industrialization' and infer Snow downplayed science's role.
distractors.why_wrong: Snow argued ONLY science could close the gap — he assigned science the central role, not a small one.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on cultural critique is to disparage humanities.
distractors.why_wrong: Snow critiqued humanities for ignoring science, not for their intellectual quality per se.

thin_entry.solution_path: Snow's ultimate concern: 'if the democracies don't modernize undeveloped countries, the Communist countries will'. The cultural divide mattered because it left capable minds out of science — undermining geopolitical competition. Answer B.
thin_entry.technique: When the passage names Snow's concern as geopolitical urgency, the right answer captures the political-context relevance.

question.prompt: What is the writer’s main impression of Snow’s book?

options.text: It is difficult to understand the controversy it once gave rise to.
Its political and moral message has lost little of its original force.
It is now noteworthy for other reasons than in the mid-20th century.
Its view of the split between different fields of research remains valid.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read the writer's mild engagement as unable to grasp the original controversy.
distractors.why_wrong: The writer cites the Leavis and Trilling responses as evidence the controversy was real — he understands it.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'political and moral message' and infer continued force.
distractors.why_wrong: The writer says it's 'irretrievably a cold war document' — the original political message has lost much force.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on the cultural-split framing is its continued validity.
distractors.why_wrong: The writer calls the split 'a very slippery thing' — it's no longer the most useful angle on the book.

thin_entry.solution_path: The writer says Snow is 'irretrievably a cold war document' but 'one of the deepest tensions in our ideas about progress' is what 'speaks most directly to us today' — the book is interesting for new reasons. Answer C.
thin_entry.technique: When the writer names the book's CURRENT interest as different from its ORIGINAL interest, the right answer captures the shift in noteworthy reasons.

question.prompt: What is argued here about the use of comics for learning science?

options.text: They can mainly provide simplified versions of scientific explanations.
Making them effective requires them to be very well designed.
Ultimately it is likely that they will mostly be used as entertainment.
They will be especially useful for those already interested in science.

question.context:
Comics in Science

There are many aspects of comics that make them a promising tool for science communication. Comics can combine engaging storytelling with the structure of diagrams, they allow us to travel into distant worlds with fictional characters, and they map abstract concepts onto more familiar ones with metaphors. A more fundamental question, however, remains to be answered: Do comics actually improve learning or engagement with science? Some research results are encouraging and suggest that the true value of comics may be engaging those readers who are less likely to seek other forms of science communication. Yet, although they may be easy to read, making good comics takes as much time and skill as making a good documentary. At the end of the day, the communication of science in comics will be only as good as the comics themselves.

Impressions

Research by Christopher Olivola and Alexander Todorov suggests that rapid judgments about the personality traits of political candidates, based solely on their appearance, can predict their electoral success. In other words, voters rely heavily on appearances when choosing which candidate to elect. Since voters need to navigate their way through the flood of information available about candidates in order to make fully informed choices, it is no surprise that they take mental shortcuts to get to their final decision. Olivola and Todorov conclude: “Getting people to overcome the influence of first impressions will not be an easy task. The speed, automaticity, and implicit nature of appearance-based trait inferences make them particularly hard to correct. Moreover, often people don’t even recognize that they are forming judgments about others from their appearances.”

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read comics as inherently simplified.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage credits comics with combining storytelling, structure, metaphor — not simplification specifically.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'engaging storytelling' and infer entertainment-only future.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage frames comics as a learning tool — not destined for pure entertainment.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a science-communication tool is preaching to the converted.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage explicitly says comics may reach 'readers who are less likely to seek other forms of science communication' — they target NEW audiences.

thin_entry.solution_path: The passage says 'making good comics takes as much time and skill as making a good documentary' and 'the communication of science in comics will be only as good as the comics themselves'. Quality requires real craft. Answer B.
thin_entry.technique: When the passage names design quality as the deciding factor for effectiveness, the right answer captures the design-craft requirement.

options.text: Overconfident people are favored over cleverlooking people in political elections.
The human brain is remarkably good at distinguishing people’s inherent characteristics.
Most modern political elections are decided entirely by the candidates’ physical appearance.
Opinions formed unawares are often more difficult to change than conscious ones.

question.context:
Comics in Science

There are many aspects of comics that make them a promising tool for science communication. Comics can combine engaging storytelling with the structure of diagrams, they allow us to travel into distant worlds with fictional characters, and they map abstract concepts onto more familiar ones with metaphors. A more fundamental question, however, remains to be answered: Do comics actually improve learning or engagement with science? Some research results are encouraging and suggest that the true value of comics may be engaging those readers who are less likely to seek other forms of science communication. At the end of the day, the communication of science in comics will be only as good as the comics themselves.

Research by Christopher Olivola and Alexander Todorov suggests that rapid judgments about the personality traits of political candidates, based solely on their appearance, can predict their electoral success. In other words, voters rely heavily on appearances when choosing which candidate to elect. Since voters need to navigate their way through the flood of information available about candidates in order to make fully informed choices, it is no surprise that they take mental shortcuts to get to their final decision. Olivola and Todorov conclude: “Getting people to overcome the influence of first impressions will not be an easy task. The speed, automaticity, and implicit nature of appearance-based trait inferences make them particularly hard to correct. Moreover, often people don’t even recognize that they are forming judgments about others from their appearances.”

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'rapid judgments' as preferring confident-looking candidates.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage doesn't compare overconfident to clever-looking — it describes general appearance-based trait inferences.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'predict electoral success' and infer brain accuracy.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage describes voters being SWAYED by appearance — not that those judgments are accurate.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on appearance-driven voting is to overstate the role.
distractors.why_wrong: Appearance influences voting heavily but voters also navigate other information — the passage doesn't say elections are decided ENTIRELY by appearance.

thin_entry.solution_path: Olivola and Todorov conclude that appearance-based judgments are 'particularly hard to correct' because they are 'speedy, automatic, and implicit' — and people 'don't even recognize that they are forming judgments about others from their appearances'. Unconscious opinions resist change. Answer D.
thin_entry.technique: When the passage names judgments as 'speedy, automatic, implicit' and 'hard to correct', the right answer captures unconscious opinions' resistance to change.

question.prompt: What is the basic message in the first two paragraphs?

options.text: The demand for books has long been in a state of gradual decline.
The emotional image of bookstores can now be relegated to the past.
The transformation of the book market will be the end of the paper book.
The death of the bookstore seems to have been greatly exaggerated.

question.context:
Indie Bookstores

For years, bookstores have been the repository of, along with books, a lot of highly romantic feelings. They crop up in fictional settings rather more often than their retail peers; the list of examples is long and charming, and includes the Shop Around the Corner (You’ve Got Mail), the Travel Book Co. (Notting Hill) and Women and Women First (Portlandia). Part of that appeal lies in the sense that bookstores, especially independent ones, belong to a bygone era – there’s a delicious moribund melancholy about them. Last chance to see. Ironically, that reputation may have contributed to an unexpected plot twist – independent bookstores are actually really healthy. In May of 2016, the American Booksellers Association informed a grieving public that the year before, the number of its member stores had actually increased, from 1,712 to 1,775. Counting multiple locations, the total climbed to 2,311. You can’t even call it a fluke because that was the seventh straight year it happened. The numbers are growing because business is growing. Independent-bookstore sales were up around 5% in the first four months of 2016. Indies accounted for about 10% of all books sold in 2015, up from 7% the year before. This isn’t a reflection of good news across the broader book business: the number of Barnes & Nobles stores has shrunk from 726 in 2009 to 640 in 2016, and sales at the chain have slipped every year since 2012. It’s also not true in the U.K., where the number of independent bookstores (sorry, bookshops) shrank 3% in 2015. But apparently Americans like indie bookstores, and they like buying things there. The revival of the neighborhood bookstore has a few different causes. Some are prosaic: new technology makes things like accounting and inventory management easier for small stores. The growth of social media makes it easier to promote events. The demise of the Borders chain in 2011 had the effect, in some markets, of taking competitive pressure off indies. But there are other, less tangible reasons, too. When Brian Lampkin followed his wife’s medical practice to Greensboro, N.C., he felt the lack of an indie bookstore downtown. “I just have this prejudice,” he says, “that every city needs a really good independent bookstore.” So he did something about it: he renovated a beautiful old

brick building dating from 1898, and in 2013 he opened Scuppernong Books there; the bookstore is paired with a wine-and-coffee café in the same space. Scuppernong stocks a literary-leaning list. “We’re letting Amazon and Barnes & Noble take care of the best sellers,” Lampkin says. “Where are you going to get poetry? Some Barnes & Nobles you walk into, you’re lucky to find Emily Dickinson.” The store now has a staff of eight. Businesses like Scuppernong are also benefiting from a surprise twist in the story of the e-book. After Amazon launched the Kindle in 2007, e-books began a relentless conquest of the book market, from 9% of unit sales in 2010 to 28% in 2013, at which point their eventual dominance began to feel like technological manifest destiny. But the paper book – a piece of information technology that has, after all, been tested and honed over the past 2,000 years – has declined to give way that easily. In 2015, the share of e-books (at least the non-self-published kind) actually receded to 24%. The book market appears to have rebalanced itself into a complex mix of paper and digital, with neither format completely dominating, and plenty of room for brick-and-mortar retailers. It’s becoming apparent that just as paper books turned out to have advantages over e-books, reality has its good points when compared with its virtual, two-dimensional shadow. Book-industry analysts talk a lot about “discovery,” by which they mean the way people find and purchase new books. It turns out, according to consumer research, that the best method for book discovery is still standing in a roomful of books and browsing – ahead even of click-tracking, data-mining if-you-liked-thisyou’ll-like-that algorithms. No question, indie bookstores face challenges. Theirs is not a huge growth business. No one’s getting rich. “The most surprising thing is how many times people just say thank you,” Brian Lampkin says. “I think they sort of get that there’s no real reason to do this other than love and commitment.” Even as indies gain ground, online retailers are booming, too: their share of the book market was up 5% in 2015. Amazon opened a brick-and-mortar bookstore in Seattle the same year, announcing a second one, in San Diego.

Lev Grossman, Time

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'bygone era' as gradual decline.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage notes that the actual numbers are GROWING — long-decline is the wrong story.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'romantic feelings' and infer dismissing the emotion as outdated.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage doesn't say the emotional image is now obsolete — it just notes that the death is exaggerated.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on book-market change is the end of paper.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage's whole point is paper books AND indie bookstores are alive — not the end of paper.

thin_entry.solution_path: The first two paragraphs reveal 'an unexpected plot twist – independent bookstores are actually really healthy' — the death narrative was overdone. Answer D.
thin_entry.technique: When the passage's main twist is 'reports of death exaggerated', the right answer names that exaggeration.

question.prompt: What is argued in relation to independent bookstores?

options.text: Their dominant market share has even taken some experts by surprise.
They may well be heading for a brilliant future on a global scale.
Their prospects seem to vary a great deal between countries.
They are still a long way from realizing their full market potential.

No one’s getting rich. “The most surprising thing is how many times people just say thank you,” Brian Lampkin says. “I think they sort of get that there’s no real reason to do this other than love and commitment.” Even as indies gain ground, online retailers are booming, too: their share of the book market was up 5% in 2015. distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'about 10% of all books sold' as dominant share.
distractors.why_wrong: 10% is a notable but minority share — not dominant.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'seven straight years' growth and project global brilliance.
distractors.why_wrong: The UK decline contradicts this — global brilliance isn't the story.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on emerging-market framing is unmet potential.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage doesn't claim there's much untapped market — it celebrates current growth.

thin_entry.solution_path: The passage contrasts US (growing indies) with the UK 'where the number of independent bookstores shrank 3% in 2015'. Country variation. Answer C.
thin_entry.technique: When the passage contrasts US growth with UK decline, the right answer is country-by-country variation.

question.prompt: What is implied in connection with the comeback of indie bookstores?

options.text: They fill a gap with regard to literature aimed at a more limited readership.
The increasing publication of e-books poses a serious threat to their continued existence.
Their overall profits are made possible mainly by social media exposure.
The massive competition from online booksellers will eventually put them out of business.

No one’s getting rich. “The most surprising thing is how many times people just say thank you,” Brian Lampkin says. “I think they sort of get that there’s no real reason to do this other than love and commitment.” Even as indies gain ground, online retailers are booming, too: their share of the book market was up 5% in 2015. Amazon opened a brick-and-mortar bookstore in Seattle the same year, announcing a second one, in San Diego.

Lev Grossman, Time

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to assume e-books threaten indies.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage actually describes paper books holding their own — and indie growth coincides with e-book setback.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at social media boost and infer it as the main revenue driver.
distractors.why_wrong: Social media is one of several mundane causes — the deeper reason is the niche-market fit.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on online retail share is to predict indie demise.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage notes online retailers booming AND indies gaining ground — not zero-sum competition.

thin_entry.solution_path: Lampkin says 'We're letting Amazon and Barnes & Noble take care of the best sellers. Where are you going to get poetry?' Indies serve niche readers. Answer A.
thin_entry.technique: When the passage cites a specific niche (poetry) indies cover that chains don't, the right answer captures specialised-readership service.

question.prompt: What is said as regards e-books?

options.text: They were never regarded as a serious threat to paper books.
Their takeover of the book market appears virtually unstoppable.
They will remain a marginal option to readers of serious literature.
Their continuing success story has suffered an unexpected setback.

question.context:
Indie Bookstores

For years, bookstores have been the repository of, along with books, a lot of highly romantic feelings. They crop up in fictional settings rather more often than their retail peers; the list of examples is long and charming, and includes the Shop Around the Corner (You’ve Got Mail), the Travel Book Co. (Notting Hill) and Women and Women First (Portlandia). Part of that appeal lies in the sense that bookstores, especially independent ones, belong to a bygone era – there’s a delicious moribund melancholy about them. Last chance to see. Ironically, that reputation may have contributed to an unexpected plot twist – independent bookstores are actually really healthy. In May of 2016, the American Booksellers Association informed a grieving public that the year before, the number of its member stores had actually increased, from 1,712 to 1,775. Counting multiple locations, the total climbed to 2,311. You can’t even call it a fluke because that was the seventh straight year it happened. The numbers are growing because business is growing. Independent-bookstore sales were up around 5% in the first four months of 2016. Indies accounted for about 10% of all books sold in 2015, up from 7% the year before. This isn’t a reflection of good news across the broader book business: the number of Barnes & Nobles stores has shrunk from 726 in 2009 to 640 in 2016, and sales at the chain have slipped every year since 2012. It’s also not true in the U.K., where the number of independent bookstores (sorry, bookshops) shrank 3% in 2015. But apparently Americans like indie bookstores, and they like buying things there. The revival of the neighborhood bookstore has a few different causes. Some are prosaic: new technology makes things like accounting and inventory management easier for small stores. The growth of social media makes it easier to promote events. The demise of the Borders chain in 2011 had the effect, in some markets, of taking competitive pressure off indies. But there are other, less tangible reasons, too. When Brian Lampkin followed his wife’s medical practice to Greensboro, N.C., he felt the lack of an indie bookstore downtown. “I just have this prejudice,” he says, “that every city needs a really good independent bookstore.” So he did something about it: he renovated a beautiful old

brick building dating from 1898, and in 2013 he opened Scuppernong Books there; the bookstore is paired with a wine-and-coffee café in the same space. Scuppernong stocks a literary-leaning list. “We’re letting Amazon and Barnes & Noble take care of the best sellers,” Lampkin says. “Where are you going to get poetry? Some Barnes & Nobles you walk into, you’re lucky to find Emily Dickinson.” The store now has a staff of eight. Businesses like Scuppernong are also benefiting from a surprise twist in the story of the e-book. After Amazon launched the Kindle in 2007, e-books began a relentless conquest of the book market, from 9% of unit sales in 2010 to 28% in 2013, at which point their eventual dominance began to feel like technological manifest destiny. But the paper book – a piece of information technology that has, after all, been tested and honed over the past 2,000 years – has declined to give way that easily. In 2015, the share of e-books (at least the non-self-published kind) actually receded to 24%. The book market appears to have rebalanced itself into a complex mix of paper and digital, with neither format completely dominating, and plenty of room for brick-and-mortar retailers. It’s becoming apparent that just as paper books turned out to have advantages over e-books, reality has its good points when compared with its virtual, two-dimensional shadow. Book-industry analysts talk a lot about “discovery,” by which they mean the way people find and purchase new books. It turns out, according to consumer research, that the best method for book discovery is still standing in a roomful of books and browsing – ahead even of click-tracking, data-mining if-you-liked-thisyou’ll-like-that algorithms. No question, indie bookstores face challenges. Theirs is not a huge growth business. No one’s getting rich. “The most surprising thing is how many times people just say thank you,” Brian Lampkin says. “I think they sort of get that there’s no real reason to do this other than love and commitment.” Even as indies gain ground, online retailers are booming, too: their share of the book market was up 5% in 2015. Amazon opened a brick-and-mortar bookstore in Seattle the same year, announcing a second one, in San Diego.

Lev Grossman, Time

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read e-book share growth as never a threat.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage explicitly says e-book dominance 'began to feel like technological manifest destiny' — threat was perceived.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the initial growth and predict unstoppable takeover.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage's whole twist is that growth STOPPED — takeover wasn't unstoppable.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on e-book setback is literary niche resistance.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage describes e-books at 24% — that's far from marginal.

thin_entry.solution_path: E-books rose from 9% (2010) to 28% (2013), then 'receded to 24%' by 2015 — a setback after years of conquest. Answer D.
thin_entry.technique: When the passage names a KULMEN-then-retreat trajectory, the right answer captures the unexpected setback.

question.prompt: What is concluded at the end of the text about indie bookstores?

options.text: The long-term profitability of their business strategy is beyond doubt.
In simplistic terms, they do not seem to be in it just for the money.
They may be seen as a temporary feature of the book market.
Recently, they have turned into an increasingly lucrative business.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'gain ground' as a profitable strategy.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage says explicitly 'no one's getting rich' — profitability isn't beyond doubt.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the closing online-retail boom and predict indie demise.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage's overall message is indie survival — not as a temporary blip.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on rising sales is lucrative business.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage celebrates indie GROWTH but says no one's getting rich — modest, not lucrative.

thin_entry.solution_path: The closing: 'No one's getting rich' and Lampkin notes 'there's no real reason to do this other than love and commitment'. The motivation is non-financial. Answer B.
thin_entry.technique: When the passage closes with 'no one's getting rich' and 'love and commitment', the right answer captures non-pecuniary motivation.

question.prompt: What is the main argument in this text?

options.text: Constantly changing recipes may appeal to new groups of consumers.
Developing tastes give rise to the acceptance of foreign kinds of dishes.
Improved varieties of traditional food are only loosely linked to the original.
Modified dishes may give indirect clues about the people who eat them.

question.context:
Food for Thought

As a dish is handed down the generations, passed between cultures, or shipped abroad, its recipe is reinscribed by all those who eat it. At first, the differences might seem small, but over time it can change out of all recognition. New ingredients are substituted for old, new methods of preparation are adopted and, every now and then, new names are bestowed. But while the accretion of such alterations may obscure the original, each new ‘layer’ tells us something about a different group of consumers: their tastes, their economy, their language and their relationship with those from whom they adopted the dish. This is particularly true of Anglo-Indian cuisine, where, of course, the most obvious examples are curry and mulligatawny soup.

Breaking Up

Few animals appear more affectionate than the blackbrowed albatross. These large seabirds, whose dark eyebrows shadow their eyes like mascara, are socially monogamous and often mate for life. Their romanticseeming “marriages” have a practical purpose: staying with the same partner builds trust, which is essential as the pair alternates between lengthy foraging trips and egg-incubation duties. But “divorce” is not unheard of. As is the case with other monogamous animals, a female albatross will leave a partnership that lacks breeding success. The process is relatively understated and free from noisy squabbles, says University of Lisbon biologist Francesco Ventura. Often when a female deems the partnership unsuccessful over the course of a year, she will simply appear with a different male in the following breeding season.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'passed between cultures' as appealing to consumers.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage doesn't argue for the appeal-strategy of changing recipes — it argues for what the changes REVEAL.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the cross-cultural framing and infer taste evolution.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage doesn't track foreign-food acceptance — it tracks what adaptations tell us about adopters.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on recipe evolution is improvement.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage doesn't call the modifications improvements — only that they accumulate, sometimes obscuring origins.

thin_entry.solution_path: The passage says each new layer of recipe adaptation 'tells us something about a different group of consumers: their tastes, their economy, their language and their relationship'. Modified dishes reveal their eaters. Answer D.
thin_entry.technique: When the passage names recipe changes as evidence about their consumers, the right answer captures the diagnostic function.

question.prompt: What is implied here about black-browed albatross couples?

options.text: They separate in a less dramatic fashion than humans do.
They seem to have less stable partnerships than most other birds.
Their mating patterns have not yet been fully understood.
Their partnerships tend to be more temporary than those of humans.

question.context:
Food for Thought

As a dish is handed down the generations, passed between cultures, or shipped abroad, its recipe is reinscribed by all those who eat it. Their romanticseeming “marriages” have a practical purpose: staying with the same partner builds trust, which is essential as the pair alternates between lengthy foraging trips and egg-incubation duties. But “divorce” is not unheard of. As is the case with other monogamous animals, a female albatross will leave a partnership that lacks breeding success. The process is relatively understated and free from noisy squabbles, says University of Lisbon biologist Francesco Ventura. Often when a female deems the partnership unsuccessful over the course of a year, she will simply appear with a different male in the following breeding season.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'divorce is not unheard of' as instability.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage says they 'often mate for life' — partnerships are quite stable; divorce is the exception.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'not unheard of' and infer ongoing mystery.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage gives a clear behavioural account — the female 'simply appears with a different male' the next season.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on bird partnerships is short-term.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage stresses 'often mate for life' — their partnerships are typically longer than human ones, not more temporary.

thin_entry.solution_path: The passage says 'the process is relatively understated and free from noisy squabbles' — albatross divorce is less dramatic than the human equivalent. Answer A.
thin_entry.technique: When the passage names albatross separation as 'understated and free from noisy squabbles', the right answer is less dramatic than human.

question.prompt: What can be concluded from the first two paragraphs?

options.text: Cats in Europe seem overprotected compared to cats in the US.
The negative impact of cats has so far received limited attention in Britain.
European cat owners have deliberately ignored the risks to other animals.
Americans tend to exaggerate the figures relating to the harm done by cats.

question.context:
Killer Cats

In the US, the debate about free-roaming cats has been raging for years. Research has shown that cats – mostly feral but also pets – kill billions of birds and mammals every year. And while it’s primarily motivated by perceived risks from coyotes and traffic, 70% of US cat owners now keep their cats inside, up from 35% in the late 1990s. UK cat owners feel differently: about 70% let their cats out, a similar percentage to other European countries. Access to the great outdoors is considered good for cats’ wellbeing, a position shared by charities such as Cats Protection, and there are few predators to worry about. The EU has even declared that it believes in the free movement of cats. “It’s a massive societal difference [from the US],” says Robbie McDonald, a wildlife ecologist at the University of Exeter. But cats are prolific hunters of wildlife in the UK and Europe, too. A study based on the 2011 pet population estimates that UK cats kill 160 to 270 million animals annually, a quarter of them birds. The real figure is likely to be even higher, boosted by the pandemic pet craze. So is it time to reconsider the British laissez-faire attitude to free-roaming cats? It is well established that cats can have serious impacts on wildlife and even cause extinctions. The most famous example is Lyall’s wren, a small flightless bird that once lived on Stephens Island, New Zealand. When Europeans arrived on the island in the late 1800s, they brought their cats – within the year, Lyall’s wren was gone. In the UK and Europe, however, cat invasions happened a long time ago. Cats were brought from the Fertile Crescent to south-eastern Europe several thousand years ago, and then introduced to Britain by the Romans. “We’ve had a much longer history of dealing with any impact that they may have had,” says Philip Baker, a mammologist at the University of Reading. Though the relationship between how many cats live in an area and how many birds they take is not straightforward, there is evidence that when cat numbers go up, so do cat kills. A large 2019 study found that the number of garden birds killed by cats in France and Belgium has increased by more than 50% in 15 years, alongside an increase in those countries’ pet cat populations. However, not everyone is convinced that cats are actually putting bird populations under pressure. According to Philip Baker, the birds most hunted by cats have

so many young that they can afford to lose a lot of them. The UK’s biggest bird charity, the Royal Society for the Protection of Birds, is not particularly concerned about the impact of cats on the British mainland. Instead, it focuses on what it says is driving UK bird declines: global warming, intensive agriculture and expanding towns and cities leading to habitat and food loss. A big reason why it is less worried is the evidence that cats primarily take “the doomed surplus”: weak or injured birds likely to die anyway. Although Philip Baker sees no evidence that cats are responsible for UK bird declines, he agrees that there is a problem worth discussing. “Cats are one of the biggest welfare issues for wildlife in this country,” he says. So should the British adopt the American way and ditch free roaming? Though it is early days, a growing minority are keeping their cats fully indoors. And that worries Daniel Cummings from Cats Protection. “We’re not saying that all cats need to go outside,” he says, but if indoor-only became the norm, some cats “would have significantly reduced welfare”. Instead, Cats Protection recommends a dusk-tilldawn cat curfew. Cats and birds are most active at dusk and dawn. “It’s a sensible compromise,” says Cummings. “You’re effectively reducing the risk of [the cats] getting in a road traffic collision overnight, and also reducing the risk of predation.” Tara Pirie, a cat ecologist from the University of Reading who has studied wild leopards in South Africa, is also conflicted about confining cats permanently. She thinks cat-free buffer zones around conservation areas could be a good idea, but that the most important thing is to listen to people’s concerns. “Most people who own cats are animal people,” she says. “They don’t want to have this impact on wildlife either.” But from the perspective of Peter Marra, an ecologist at Georgetown University in the US and one of the authors of the 2016 book Cat Wars, none of this goes far enough. “I don’t think there should be a compromise,” he says. “It’s about people taking responsibility for their animal.” What that means, however, might prove to be a cultural difference.

Ida Emilie Steinmark, The Guardian Weekly

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'wellbeing' as cats being overprotected.
distractors.why_wrong: The UK position prioritises CAT freedom, not protection — wildlife protection is what's missing.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'EU declared belief in free movement' and infer deliberate dismissal.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage doesn't say Europeans CONSCIOUSLY ignored risks — only that risks haven't been the central concern.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on cross-country differences is to suspect data inflation.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage doesn't say American figures are exaggerated — it cites US research approvingly.

thin_entry.solution_path: The passage contrasts US owners (70% keep cats indoors after wildlife concerns) with UK owners (70% let them out) — and notes the UK position 'considered good for cats' wellbeing'. UK has paid limited attention to cat impact on wildlife. Answer B.
thin_entry.technique: When the passage contrasts US wildlife concern with UK cat-wellbeing focus, the right answer captures British under-attention to wildlife impact.

question.prompt: What is said about cats in the UK and Europe?

options.text: They apparently cause less harm to other animals than American cats.
Their hunting behaviour has been modified by their long history as pets.
They are no less dangerous to other animals than are American cats.
Their owners have managed to domesticate them in different ways.

Ida Emilie Steinmark, The Guardian Weekly

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'much longer history' as adaptation reducing harm.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage compares LONGER coexistence (so wildlife may have adapted) but cats themselves haven't become less dangerous.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the long history and infer modified behaviour.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage doesn't claim cats hunt less now — only that European wildlife has had more time to deal with them.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on cultural differences is varying domestication.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage describes the same species behaving similarly — the variation is in OWNER PRACTICE, not cat domestication.

thin_entry.solution_path: The passage says 'cats are prolific hunters of wildlife in the UK and Europe, too' and gives 160-270 million animals killed annually by UK cats. Equally dangerous. Answer C.
thin_entry.technique: When the passage explicitly says UK cats are 'prolific hunters... too', the right answer captures equal dangerousness.

question.prompt: What can be concluded about cats in relation to the decline of birds in Britain?

options.text: In recent years, cats have been universally blamed.
Cat owners often fail to live up to their responsibility.
Overall, cats can hardly be assigned a significant role.
Cats’ harmful effects on bird habitats are beyond doubt.

However, not everyone is convinced that cats are actually putting bird populations under pressure. According to Philip Baker, the birds most hunted by cats have

so many young that they can afford to lose a lot of them. The UK’s biggest bird charity, the Royal Society for the Protection of Birds, is not particularly concerned about the impact of cats on the British mainland. Instead, it focuses on what it says is driving UK bird declines: global warming, intensive agriculture and expanding towns and cities leading to habitat and food loss. A big reason why it is less worried is the evidence that cats primarily take “the doomed surplus”: weak or injured birds likely to die anyway. Although Philip Baker sees no evidence that cats are responsible for UK bird declines, he agrees that there is a problem worth discussing. “Cats are one of the biggest welfare issues for wildlife in this country,” he says. So should the British adopt the American way and ditch free roaming? Though it is early days, a growing minority are keeping their cats fully indoors. And that worries Daniel Cummings from Cats Protection. “We’re not saying that all cats need to go outside,” he says, but if indoor-only became the norm, some cats “would have significantly reduced welfare”. Instead, Cats Protection recommends a dusk-tilldawn cat curfew. Cats and birds are most active at dusk and dawn. “It’s a sensible compromise,” says Cummings. “You’re effectively reducing the risk of [the cats] getting in a road traffic collision overnight, and also reducing the risk of predation.” Tara Pirie, a cat ecologist from the University of Reading who has studied wild leopards in South Africa, is also conflicted about confining cats permanently. She thinks cat-free buffer zones around conservation areas could be a good idea, but that the most important thing is to listen to people’s concerns. “Most people who own cats are animal people,” she says. “They don’t want to have this impact on wildlife either.” But from the perspective of Peter Marra, an ecologist at Georgetown University in the US and one of the authors of the 2016 book Cat Wars, none of this goes far enough. “I don’t think there should be a compromise,” he says. “It’s about people taking responsibility for their animal.” What that means, however, might prove to be a cultural difference.

Ida Emilie Steinmark, The Guardian Weekly

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read the public debate as universal blame.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage shows the RSPB and Baker both downplaying cats' role in bird declines — blame isn't universal.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the controversy and infer owner failures.
distractors.why_wrong: The question is about cats' role in bird decline, not about owner responsibility — and cats are exonerated for declines.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on cat impact is harmful habitat effect.
distractors.why_wrong: Cats kill individual birds, but the passage attributes habitat loss to humans (climate, agriculture, urbanisation), not cats.

thin_entry.solution_path: RSPB 'is not particularly concerned about the impact of cats on the British mainland' and points to climate, agriculture, urbanisation as drivers. Cats take 'the doomed surplus' — not a significant role. Answer C.
thin_entry.technique: When the passage names cats taking 'the doomed surplus' and other factors as bird-decline drivers, the right answer downgrades cat role.

question.prompt: What are we told about the debate on cats in Britain?

options.text: The current trend seems to be heading towards less freedom for cats to spend time outdoors.
On the whole, there appears to be little interest in the basic issues involved.
Most cat-owners are worried about the problem of free-roaming cats.
In general, cat lovers are dead against restricting their pets’ movement in and out of the house.

question.context:
Killer Cats

In the US, the debate about free-roaming cats has been raging for years. Research has shown that cats – mostly feral but also pets – kill billions of birds and mammals every year. And while it’s primarily motivated by perceived risks from coyotes and traffic, 70% of US cat owners now keep their cats inside, up from 35% in the late 1990s. UK cat owners feel differently: about 70% let their cats out, a similar percentage to other European countries. Access to the great outdoors is considered good for cats’ wellbeing, a position shared by charities such as Cats Protection, and there are few predators to worry about. The EU has even declared that it believes in the free movement of cats. “It’s a massive societal difference [from the US],” says Robbie McDonald, a wildlife ecologist at the University of Exeter. But cats are prolific hunters of wildlife in the UK and Europe, too. A study based on the 2011 pet population estimates that UK cats kill 160 to 270 million animals annually, a quarter of them birds. The real figure is likely to be even higher, boosted by the pandemic pet craze. So is it time to reconsider the British laissez-faire attitude to free-roaming cats? It is well established that cats can have serious impacts on wildlife and even cause extinctions. The most famous example is Lyall’s wren, a small flightless bird that once lived on Stephens Island, New Zealand. When Europeans arrived on the island in the late 1800s, they brought their cats – within the year, Lyall’s wren was gone. In the UK and Europe, however, cat invasions happened a long time ago. Cats were brought from the Fertile Crescent to south-eastern Europe several thousand years ago, and then introduced to Britain by the Romans. “We’ve had a much longer history of dealing with any impact that they may have had,” says Philip Baker, a mammologist at the University of Reading. Though the relationship between how many cats live in an area and how many birds they take is not straightforward, there is evidence that when cat numbers go up, so do cat kills. A large 2019 study found that the number of garden birds killed by cats in France and Belgium has increased by more than 50% in 15 years, alongside an increase in those countries’ pet cat populations. However, not everyone is convinced that cats are actually putting bird populations under pressure. According to Philip Baker, the birds most hunted by cats have

so many young that they can afford to lose a lot of them. The UK’s biggest bird charity, the Royal Society for the Protection of Birds, is not particularly concerned about the impact of cats on the British mainland. Instead, it focuses on what it says is driving UK bird declines: global warming, intensive agriculture and expanding towns and cities leading to habitat and food loss. A big reason why it is less worried is the evidence that cats primarily take “the doomed surplus”: weak or injured birds likely to die anyway. Although Philip Baker sees no evidence that cats are responsible for UK bird declines, he agrees that there is a problem worth discussing. “Cats are one of the biggest welfare issues for wildlife in this country,” he says. So should the British adopt the American way and ditch free roaming? Though it is early days, a growing minority are keeping their cats fully indoors. And that worries Daniel Cummings from Cats Protection. “We’re not saying that all cats need to go outside,” he says, but if indoor-only became the norm, some cats “would have significantly reduced welfare”. Instead, Cats Protection recommends a dusk-tilldawn cat curfew. Cats and birds are most active at dusk and dawn. “It’s a sensible compromise,” says Cummings. “You’re effectively reducing the risk of [the cats] getting in a road traffic collision overnight, and also reducing the risk of predation.” Tara Pirie, a cat ecologist from the University of Reading who has studied wild leopards in South Africa, is also conflicted about confining cats permanently. She thinks cat-free buffer zones around conservation areas could be a good idea, but that the most important thing is to listen to people’s concerns. “Most people who own cats are animal people,” she says. “They don’t want to have this impact on wildlife either.” But from the perspective of Peter Marra, an ecologist at Georgetown University in the US and one of the authors of the 2016 book Cat Wars, none of this goes far enough. “I don’t think there should be a compromise,” he says. “It’s about people taking responsibility for their animal.” What that means, however, might prove to be a cultural difference.

Ida Emilie Steinmark, The Guardian Weekly

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'British laissez-faire' as continued apathy.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage describes active discussion, indoor-cat trend, dusk-dawn curfew advice — interest is rising.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the wildlife framing and assume widespread owner worry.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage says 'a growing minority' — most owners aren't worried, but some are.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on cat lovers is resistance to restriction.
distractors.why_wrong: Cat lovers are described as conflicted — they care about wildlife impact too, not 'dead against restriction'.

thin_entry.solution_path: The passage says 'a growing minority are keeping their cats fully indoors' and discusses British movements toward restricting cat freedom (curfews, indoor-only). Trend toward less outdoor freedom. Answer A.
thin_entry.technique: When the passage names a growing minority restricting outdoor access, the right answer captures the trend toward less freedom.

question.prompt: How can the closing remarks on cats in Britain and America best be summarised?

options.text: There is profound disagreement on the environmental harm done by cats.
British people at large appear more ignorant of cats’ impact than Americans.
There is a broad consensus as regards cats’ need to be allowed outdoors.
Clear differences are to be found between British and American attitudes.

question.context:

Instead, Cats Protection recommends a dusk-tilldawn cat curfew. She thinks cat-free buffer zones around conservation areas could be a good idea, but that the most important thing is to listen to people’s concerns. “Most people who own cats are animal people,” she says. “They don’t want to have this impact on wildlife either.” But from the perspective of Peter Marra, an ecologist at Georgetown University in the US and one of the authors of the 2016 book Cat Wars, none of this goes far enough. “I don’t think there should be a compromise,” he says. “It’s about people taking responsibility for their animal.” What that means, however, might prove to be a cultural difference.

Ida Emilie Steinmark, The Guardian Weekly

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read the closing as deep disagreement on environmental harm.
distractors.why_wrong: Both sides ACKNOWLEDGE cats hunt wildlife — the disagreement is on what to do about it, not on the harm itself.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the British downplay-the-impact framing and infer ignorance.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage describes British discussion as informed — Baker and RSPB engage seriously, not from ignorance.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on cat owners is shared belief in outdoor access.
distractors.why_wrong: The US 70% indoor and UK 70% outdoor split shows the OPPOSITE of consensus — clear cultural divide.

thin_entry.solution_path: The closing quotes Peter Marra in the US calling for no compromise while UK voices propose curfews and indoor-cat options. 'It's a cultural difference'. Clear differences between US and UK attitudes. Answer D.
thin_entry.technique: When the closing names the difference as 'a cultural difference', the right answer captures the British-American attitude divide.
